coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen peroxide assisted synthesis of sulfur quantum dots for the detection of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Qin
An, Xuanxuan
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), heavy-metal-free quantum dots, are regarded as the next generation promising green nanomaterials compared with traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots. However, there have been few reports on the synthesis and application of SQDs for analytical detection. Herein, an H2O2-assisted top-down method is used to synthesize SQDs. The as-obtained SQDs have good water dispersion, stability, photoluminescence (PL) properties and achieving a quantum yield (QY) to 11%. After adding Cr (VI) in SQDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases base on inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, Cr (VI) can be reduced to Cr(III) when ascorbic acid (AA) is introduced into the SQDs - Cr (VI) system, accompanying the recovery of the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence sensor displays high sensitivity and quickly response toward Cr (VI) and AA in a range of 10–120 μmol L−1 and 20–500 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.36 μmol L−1 and 1.21 μmol L−1, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence sensor has been applied for the determination of Cr (VI) and AA in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
IFE
Chromium (VI)
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrogen peroxide assisted synthesis of SQDs
The fluorescence sensing for Cr (VI) and AA
Selectivity of Cr (VI) and interference of other substances on Cr (VI) and AA detection
Analysis of Cr (VI) and AA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of SQDs. (B) The size distribution histogram of SQDs. (C) XRD of SQDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of SQDs. (E) XPS spectra of SQDs. (F) The XPS spectrum of SQDs. High resolution spectra of S 2p.
(A) The UV absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SQDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of SQDs at different excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm.
The effects of various conditions on the fluorescence intensity of SQDs (A) ionic strengths. (B) pH.

Optimization of conditions. (A) Effects of PBS buffer solution with different pH (5.8, 6.2, 6.6, 7.0, 7.4, 7.8, 0.2 mol L−1) on fluorescence intensity of SQDs and SQDs - Cr (VI) system. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 50 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 875 μL; time, 5 min. (B) Effects of PBS buffer solution with different pH (5.8, 6.2, 6.6, 7.0, 7.4, 7.8, 0.2 mol L−1) on fluorescence intensity of SQDs - Cr (VI) and SQDs - Cr (VI) - AA system. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 50 μmol L−1; AA, 125 μL, 500 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 750 μL; time, 5 min. (C) The response time for Cr (VI). Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 50 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 875 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1. (D) The reaction time for AA. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 50 μmol L−1; AA, 125 μL, 500 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 750 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of SQDs in the presence of different concentration of Cr (VI) (0, 10, 15, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100,120 μmol L−1). (B) Calibration curve for Cr (VI). Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 10–120 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 875 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1. (C) Different concentrations of AA (0, 20, 40, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400,500 μmol L−1) restoredthe fluorescence of the SQDs - Cr (VI) system. (D) Calibration curve for AA. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 125 μL, 100 μmol L−1; AA, 125 μL, 20–500 μmol L−1; PBS buffer solution, 750 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1.

(A) UV absorption spectra of SQDs-Cr (VI), Cr (VI), H2O2 and H2O2-Cr (VI). (B) UV absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) and fluorescence excitation, emission spectrums of SQDs. (C) The fluorescence lifetime of SQDs and SQDs + Cr (VI).

(A) The selectivity of Cr (VI). Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 100 μmol L−1; different ions, 100 μmol L−1; pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of SQDs - Cr (VI) system in the presence of diverse interfering ions. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 100 μmol L−1; different ions, 1000 μmol L−1; pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1. (C) Relative fluorescence intensity of SQDs - Cr (VI) -AA system in the presence of diverse interference substances. Conditions: SQDs, 250 μL; Cr (VI), 100 μmol L−1; AA, 500 μmol L−1; interference substances, 5000 μmol L−1; pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1.
(A) The synthesis of SQDs. (B) Schematic illustration of principle of the Cr (VI) and AA assay.
The different detection methods for Cr (VI) and AA.
Determination of Cr (VI) in water samples and AA in fruit juice samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Hydrogen peroxide assisted synthesis of sulfur quantum dots for the detection of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), heavy-metal-free quantum dots, are regarded as the next generation promising green nanomaterials compared with traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots. However, there have been few reports on the synthesis and application of SQDs for analytical detection. Herein, an H2O2-assisted top-down method is used to synthesize SQDs. The as-obtained SQDs have good water dispersion, stability, photoluminescence (PL) properties and achieving a quantum yield (QY) to 11%. After adding Cr (VI) in SQDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases base on inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, Cr (VI) can be reduced to Cr(III) when ascorbic acid (AA) is introduced into the SQDs - Cr (VI) system, accompanying the recovery of the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence sensor displays high sensitivity and quickly response toward Cr (VI) and AA in a range of 10–120 μmol L−1 and 20–500 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.36 μmol L−1 and 1.21 μmol L−1, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence sensor has been applied for the determination of Cr (VI) and AA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various heavy metals ions, chromium is widely applied to a diverse range of industries like leathertanning, metallurgy, chrome plating, lumbering industry [1,2]. Chromium exists in two main forms in environment: trivalent (Cr (III)) and hexavalent (Cr (VI)). Cr (VI) is known as highly toxic species and is far more toxic than Cr (III) and restricted by the Dangerous Substances Directive (67/548/EEC) [3]. Due to its high oxidation state and ability to penetrate biofilms, World Health Organization (WHO) has asserted the maximum amount of Cr (VI) is limited 0.05 mg L−1 [4–6]. Until now, various techniques have been applied for Cr (VI) detection, including atomic adsorption spectroscopy [7], electrochemical analysis [8], ion-chromatography [9], inductively coupled plasa-mass spectrometry [10]. Many of these techniques require high cost, time-consuming and substantial operational skills. Thus, it is eager to explore low cost, fast response, facile sensor to accurately quantify Cr (VI).

Ascorbic acid (AA) is an important antioxidant and is widely found in fresh vegetables, fruits and liver of animals [11–14]. However, AA cannot be synthesized in human, primates, marmots and a few other animals, which must be through food, drugs, and other ingestion [15]. The intake of AA is strongly associated with of the common cold, scurvy, mental illness, diarrhea, cancer, AIDS and infertility [12,16–20]. Therefore, using simple, convenient, highly fluorescent, eco-friendly and inexpensive nanomaterials to detect AA are critical to food quality and health care.

Luminescent quantum dots, due to their unique size and structure, have emerged as promising applications in catalysis, analysis, biological labeling, and medicine [21–23]. However, heavy-metal-based quantum dots (for example, Cd, Hg, and Pb) have potential problems of toxicity and environmental harm, which limits their applications [24–27]. Sulfur nanomaterials with low toxicity, good solubility and stable luminescent properties have obtained extraordinary attention [28–30]. Moreover, due to natural antibacterial properties and inherent biological activities sulfur nanomaterials are superior to carbon nanomaterials and often serve as antimicrobial or antifungal agents [31]. SQDs, one of the sulfur nanomaterials, as a fluorescence sensor has been rarely reported. Consequently, there is a strong drive to expand the utilization of SQDs in novel applications.

To date, only a few researches reported the synthesis of SQDs with good PL property and the use of SQDs as sensor [32,33]. Herein, we reported a simple procedure to synthesize SQDs via hydrogen peroxide assisted method (Scheme 1 A). Under an alkaline and polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) as passivation agent environment, the sublimated sulfur powder was dissolved into small particles of sulfur dots (S dots). SQDs were obtained by injection of H2O2 led to etching of S dots. The newly prepared SQDs had produced blue emission with a PL QY of 11%. When introduce Cr (VI), the fluorescence intensity was weakened because of IFE. After adding AA into the SQDs – Cr (VI) system, the fluorescence intensity gradually recovered because Cr (VI) was reduced. Thus, based on changes in fluorescence intensity, SQDs could detection of Cr (VI) as well as AA (Scheme 1B).

Sublimed sulfur, ascorbic acid (AA) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). PEG-400 was obtained from Macklin Biochemical (Shanghai, Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Oriental Reagent Factory (Sichuan, China). Sodium hydroxide and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Factory (Chongqing, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence properties were obtained on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of SQDs were recorded on UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Microstructure of SQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, US) at 200 kv and AXS-D8 X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Primary functional groups on the surface of SQDs were performed on infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Tokyo, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) was used to characterizethe chemical composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide assisted synthesis of SQDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previously reported study, SQDs were synthesized via a top-down methodology with mildly modification [34]. Briefly, 1.4 g of sublimed sulfur, 4.0 g of sodium hydroxide and 3.0 mL of PEG-400 were added in 50.0 mL of ultrapure water. This mixture was heated at oil bath of 70 °C for 72 h under constant stirring condition. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min to get rid of large particles. 1.5 mL of the as-obtained products was mixed with 2.0 mL of H2O2 (7.5 wt%) solution in a 10 mL centrifuge tube under stirring for 10 min. Then, 1000 Da dialysis bag was used to get rid of unreacted reagents from the supernatant underwent. In the end, the solution of SQDs with blue emission was stored at 4 °C for further characterization and study.

The QY of the SQDs was measured with reference to quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO 4 (QY = 54%) at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [35]. The formula used was as follows:
Φ u
=
Φ s
F u
F s
A s
A u
n u 2
n s 2

Φ was the fluorescence quantum yield, s and u were the reference standard and the SQDs. Where F was the integrated fluorescence intensity, A was the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, n was the refractive index (ns and nu are 1.33).

PBS buffer solution (875 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1), different concentrations of Cr (VI) (125 μL) and SQDs (250 μL) were added into the tube (1.5 mL). The final total volume for fluorescence detection was 1.25 mL. After mixing for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the solutions were collected. The slit widths maintained at 5 nm and the excitation wavelength remained at 360 nm throughout the experiment.

For the detection of AA, PBS buffer solution (750 μL, pH 6.6, 0.2 mol L−1), 120 μmol L−1 of Cr (VI) (125 μL), different concentrations of AA (125 μL) and SQDs (250 μL) were added into the tube (1.5 mL). The final total volume for fluorescence detection was 1.25 mL. After incubating for 5 min at room temperature, fluorescence spectra of the SQDs – Cr (VI) – AA was recorded with slit widths kept at 5 nm and an excitation at 360 nm.

The morphology and the size distribution histogram of the as-obtained SQDs were studied by TEM. SQDs were quasi-spherical in shape (Fig. 1 A) and mainly distributed in the range of approximately 1.12–3.82 nm with an average size of 2.11 nm (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1C illustrated the XRD pattern of the as-prepared SQDs. Three peaks at 18.8°, 23.0° and 25.8° were compared to JCPDS NO. 99-0100 [36]. The position and intensity of the diffraction peak of SQDs were in accord with the JCPDS NO. 99-0100 [37]. Results displayed the possibility of sulfur polycrystalline phase formation [38]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1D) was measured to explore the functional groups. The absorption bands at 3437 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of the hydroxyl groups and the H-O-H bending vibration of H2O. The characteristic peaks of PEG were observed: 2917 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, 1351 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, 951 cm−1 [39]. Generally, because of low intensity, low resolution fingerprint area signals often appear fuzzy. But in this work, the SQDs produce obvious signals in the area. The absorption peak at 638 cm−1 was the bending vibration of the S-O. The strong peak at 616 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the S-S [40]. The XPS (Fig. 1E) showed that SQDs mainly contained three elements: S, C and O. Two distinct peaks are shown in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of S (Fig. 1F). The binding energies at 168.1 eV and 169.1 eV were composed of SO3 2− (2p 2/3) and SO3 2− (2p 1/2), respectively [41,42]. The result indicated abundant sulfonate/sulfonate group attached to SQDs surface, which was consistent with previous studies.

Fig. 2 A showed fluorescence excitation, emission spectra and UV absorption spectra of SQDs. The maximum emission peak wavelength of SQDs was 440 nm under excitation wavelength at 360 nm. There was one absorption band centered at 295 nm due to the n → σ* transition of nonbonding electrons of S [43]. There was no UV absorption at 360 nm, which was attributed to injection of H2O2 led to etching of S dots [44]. This result was similar to the previously report by Wang and co-workers. The fluorescence emission spectra of the SQDs were depicted in Fig. 2B at various excitation wavelengths. With the excitation wavelengths increasing from 300 nm to 390 nm, emission peak positions had a slight redshift, suggesting the occurrence of photon reabsorption, which revealed that SQDs have an excitation-dependent PL feature [45,46]. It showed that particle size and surface state had influence on the fluorescence emission spectrum of quantum dots [47].

The stability of the as-obtained SQDs under different ionic strengths and pH was studied. The effects of different concentration of NaCl (0–1 mol L−1) on the SQDs were checked. Fig. 3 A showed that fluorescence intensity was almost unaffected by ionic strength. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of SQDs was not significantly changed at pH 5.8–7.8. To sum up, SQDs had good stability and stable fluorescence performance.

The pH and reaction time were optimized to control the experiment interference effectively and improve the detection performance. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the difference value of fluorescence quenching increased in the range of 5.8–6.6. Nevertheless, difference value in fluorescence intensity slightly changed between 6.6 and 7.8 and reached a peak at 6.6. As displayed in Fig. 4B, with the increase of alkalinity, the recovery value of fluorescence intensity decreased slightly when AA was added into the SQDs - Cr (VI) system. Although the increase of alkalinity had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity recovery, considering that the acidic conditionwas preferred toalkalineconditionin the redox reaction of Cr (VI) and AA [48]. Therefore, pH 6.6 was used as the optimal pH value for detection. As the graph of reaction time illustrated in Fig. 4C, fluorescence intensity decreased quickly and remained unchanged after 5 min, which showed SQDs had very fast response to Cr (VI). In addition, as the Fig. 4D shown, when AA was added into SQDs - Cr (VI) system, the fluorescence recovery increased quickly andtolevel off after 5 min. Result indicated that SQDs - Cr (VI) system had very fast response to AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence sensing for Cr (VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 A showed the fluorescence intensity of SQDs decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of Cr (VI), which indicated that the system was an efficient fluorescence sensor of Cr (VI). Fig. 5B demonstrated the linear relationship between the concentration of Cr (VI) and ΔF (ΔF = F 0 − F 1), the linear equation was expressed as ΔF = 214.66c + 170.52 (F 0 and F 1 referred to the fluorescence intensity of SQDs in the presence and absence of Cr (VI)) with a linear good correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9977) and the detection limit was 0.36 μmol L−1 according to the 3σ/k principle in the linear range of 10–120 μmol L−1. Table 1 listed a comparison of our work and the previous works for Cr (VI) sensing. In terms of detection limit and sensitivity, although our proposed method was not the optimal in Table 1, it was simple and fast. Thus, this method was more applicable for daily assay of Cr (VI).

Fig. 5C illustrated the response of AA to Cr (VI). The fluorescence intensity increasingly returned to the initial value following augment the concentration of AA. The concentration of AA and ΔF (ΔF = F 2 − F 1 ) were linear relation in the range of 20–500 μmol L−1 (Fig. 5D). The linear relationship was indicated as: ΔF = 447.77c + 24.74 (R2 = 0.9980) with limit detection of 1.21 μmol L−1. Here, F 1 (absence of AA) and F 2 (presence of AA) represent the fluorescence intensity of SQDs - Cr (VI) system. Furthermore, a comparison of this method with other reported methods in Table 1. The detection limit of this work was better than that of the electrochemical method, colorimetric method and high performance liquid chromatography. Although these methods had wide linear range, the sensitivity was low.

To further explore the quenching mechanism between SQDs and Cr (VI), we had been tested UV absorption spectra of SQDs - Cr (VI), Cr (VI), H2O2 and H2O2 - Cr (VI), respectively (Fig. 6 A). In addition, fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of SQDs and UV absorption spectra of Cr (VI) were studied (Fig. 6B). The UV absorption spectra of Cr (VI) had an absorption peak at 360 nm. However, when H2O2 was introduced, there was no absorption peak in the system of H2O2 - Cr (VI) at 360 nm. The UV absorption peak of SQDs - Cr (VI) appeared at 360 nm indicated that there was not a new substance to form, which not only indicated that there was no H2O2 in the SQDs (final product) but also indicated that the possible quenching mechanism was inner filter effect (IFE) [56]. The excitation and emission wavelength of SQDs were 360 nm and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. 6B). IFE was considered as possible quenching mechanism because the absorption spectra of Cr (VI) overlap partially with the excitation and emission spectra of SQDs [56]. Fluorescence lifetime was an effective parameter to distinguish between IFE. Fig. 6C showed the fluorescence attenuation curve of SQDs and SQDs - Cr (VI) and the average luminescence lifetime were calculated to be 1.92 ns (τ 0) and 1.89 ns (τ), respectively. Here, τ0 and τ were the lifetimes in the absence and presence of quencher. The process proved the fluorescence lifetime was no significant change when inducing the quench agent. To sum up, the fluorescence quenching of SQDs was put down to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Cr (VI) and interference of other substances on Cr (VI) and AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under similar experimental conditions, we had been tested a series of relevant ions (Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Al3+, S2O3 2−, Ba2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Cr (VI)) to examine the specificity of SQDs towards Cr (VI). The results indicated that the relative fluorescence intensity (F 1/F 0) of other inorganic ions lightly or had no change compared with Cr (VI) (Fig. 7 A). Here, F 0 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensity of SQDs without and with relevant ions. The results suggested that SQDs could be used as selectively detect Cr (VI).

Different ionic (Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Na+, K+, Al3+, S2O3 2−, Ba2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cr3+) was added into SQDs. The concentration of interfering substance was 1000 μmol L−1, which was 10 times concentration of Cr (VI). The fluorescence intensity changes were shown in the Fig. 7B. The results indicated that the relative fluorescence intensity (F 1 /F 0) of other inorganic ions lightly or had no change compared with Cr (VI). Here, F 0 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensity of SQDs without and with interfering ions. The results suggested that SQDs could be used as specifically recognition of Cr (VI) and the assay method have prominently selectivity. To evaluate the selectivity sensing of AA, the potential interference foreign species, covering L-cysteine, proline, arginine, histidine, alanine, L-tryptophan, serine, threonine, L-leucine, glycine, sarcosine, glucose, glutathione, sucrose, dopamine, citric acid, catechin, SO3 2−, Fe2+. The concentration of the potential interference substances was 5000 μmol L−1, which was 10-fold concentration of AA. As illustrated in Fig. 7C, the results showed that the potential interference substances had little effect on the SQDs – Cr (VI) system. Therefore, our proposed assay performed desirable selectivity for AA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cr (VI) and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the feasibility and accuracy of this method, water samples, lemon juice and kiwifruit juice were used as real samples determination of Cr (VI) and AA. The standard addition method was used to monitor water samples taken from lake water of university. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of Cr (VI) in water samples were in the range of 98.3%−102.8%, and with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.6%−2.1%, indicating that the feasibility of the method based on SQDs and no obvious system error of method. Then, the lemon juice and kiwifruit juice were chosen for survey to evaluate the practical applications in detecting AA by our method. The standard addition method was also adopted, and the test report was shown in the Table 2. The recoveries of AA in lemon juice and kiwifruit juice were between 96.2% and 101.5% with an RSD 1.2%–3.2%. This result depicted that sensor based on SQDs - Cr (VI) had a huge potential for determination of AA in real samples. Consequently, the detection method had the applicability and potential to lake water samples, the lemon juice and kiwifruit juice.

In summary, blue fluorescent SQDs with QY up to 11% could be prepared by sublimed sulfur, PEG-400 and hydrogen peroxide under an alkaline environment. The as-obtained SQDs had easily dispersed in solutions, stability and preferable photoluminescence (PL) properties. The newly prepared of SQDs via a “top-down” methodology was used for the determination of Cr (VI) and AA with high sensitivity and selectivity. Based on IFE, the fluorescence of SQDs was quenched by Cr (VI). While AA was introduced into the SQDs - Cr (VI) system, the fluorescence intensity evidently recovered because of the oxidation-reduction between Cr (VI) and AA. Moreover, this method has been successfully applied to the detection in real samples.
Qin Tan: Writing - original draft. Xuanxuan An: Data curation. Shuang Pan: Investigation. Hui Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705132) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2019TY003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Pacquiao, M.D.G. Luna, N. Thongsai, S. Kladsomboon, P. Paoprasert, Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications, Applied Surface Science 453 (2018) 192-203.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective chromium (VI) ligands identified using combinatorial peptoid libraries
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Knight, E.Y. Zhou, J.G. Pelton, M.B. Francis, Selective chromium (VI) ligands identified using combinatorial peptoid libraries, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 135 (2013) 17488-17493.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of the distribution pattern of chromium species in single cells
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wei, L.L. Hu, M.L. Chen, T. Yang, J.H. Wang, Analysis of the distribution pattern of chromium species in single cells, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 12437-12444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Accurate quantification of total chromium and its speciation form Cr (VI) in water by ICP-DRC-IDMS and HPLC/ICP-DRC-IDMS
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Markiewicz, I. Komorowicz, D. Baralkiewicz, Accurate quantification of total chromium and its speciation form Cr (VI) in water by ICP-DRC-IDMS and HPLC/ICP-DRC-IDMS, Talanta 152 (2016) 489-497.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of trace amounts of chromium (VI) using hydrogel coated fiber bragg grating
Sens. Actuators, B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. V.N. Kishore, M. Sai Shankar, M. Satyanarayana, Detection of trace amounts of chromium (VI) using hydrogel coated fiber bragg grating, Sensors and Actuators B 243 (2017) 626-633.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple colorimetric sensor for Cr (III) and Cr (VI) speciation using silver nanoparticles as a probe
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Elavarasi, A. Rajeshwari, S. A. Alex, D. N. Kumar, N. Chandrasekaran, A. Mukherjee, Simple colorimetric sensor for Cr (III) and Cr (VI) speciation using silver nanoparticles as a probe, Anal. Methods 6 (2014) 5161-5167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound assisted-deep eutectic solvent based on emulsification liquid phase microextraction combined with microsample injection flame atomic absorption spectrometry for valence speciation of chromium (III/VI) in environmental samples
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Yilmaz, M. Soylak, Ultrasound assisted-deep eutectic solvent based on emulsification liquid phase microextraction combined with microsample injection flame atomic absorption spectrometry for valence speciation of chromium (III/VI) in environmental samples, Talanta 160 (2016) 680-685.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of chromium (VI) based on gold nanoparticle-decorated titania nanotube arrays
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Jin, G. Wu, A. Chen, Sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of chromium (VI) based on gold nanoparticle-decorated titania nanotube arrays, Analyst 139 (2014) 235-241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Speciation of chromium in waste water using ion chromatography inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
